cards.back: def f(x=[]) shares one list across calls. Use x=None, then x = x or [].
is compares identity (same object); == compares value.
Lazy, O(1) memory, one at a time; no indexing, consumed once.
One thread runs bytecode at a time; CPU-bound work needs multiprocessing.
Runs __enter__/__exit__ so cleanup happens even on exceptions.
[x*2 for x in xs if x>0] — concise, fast list building.
*args = extra positional args (tuple); **kwargs = extra keyword args (dict).
Shallow shares nested objects; deepcopy duplicates everything.
f"{name} is {age}" — readable formatting that evaluates inline.
Isolates a project's packages so versions don't clash.
Loops with an index: for i, v in enumerate(xs) — cleaner than range(len()).
Pairs items from several iterables: for a, b in zip(xs, ys).
It returns a default instead of raising KeyError when the key is missing.
Empty containers, 0, "", and None are falsy; use "if items:" not "if len(items)>0".
Slices a sequence; xs[::-1] reverses, xs[:3] takes the first three.
Use "x is None", not "x == None" — identity, not equality.
try runs code, except handles errors, finally always runs (cleanup).
@deco wraps a function to add behavior (logging, caching) without changing it.
A small anonymous function: key=lambda x: x.age for sorting.
For uniqueness and fast membership tests (x in s is O(1)).

cards.front: Best way to build a string from a list?
cards.back: "".join(parts) — far faster than repeated + in a loop.
Code there runs only when the file is run directly, not when imported.
def f(x: int) -> str documents types and enables tools; they're not enforced at runtime.
{k: v for k, v in pairs} — build a dict concisely, like list comprehensions.
